executive_summary: AUD/USD trades at 0.7063 (RBA close 10 March), having touched 0.7118 intraday — the highest level since February 2023 — representing gains of +0.3% over 1 month, +5.5% over 3 months, +9.0% over 6 months, and +13.3% over one year. The dominant narrative is a hawkish RBA regime change: the February hike to 3.85% made the RBA the first G10 central bank to reverse from cuts back to hikes, with OIS pricing a terminal rate of 4.10–4.20% by year-end against a backdrop of broad USD weakness (DXY −9% YoY). The BIS REER at 110.52 sits at the 60th–65th percentile of its long-run range, and spot is within 1–2% of OECD GDP-based PPP fair value of 0.719, placing AUD near equilibrium. The key tension is between the powerful rate and growth fundamental story (2.6% GDP, 4.1% unemployment, re-accelerating core inflation at 3.4% trimmed mean) and an increasingly precarious risk profile: CFTC positioning at a 3-year maximum bullish extreme (100th percentile COT Index), options markets pricing aggressive downside hedging (25-delta risk reversal at 9-month lows), a current account deficit widening to −2.9% of GDP, and geopolitical risk from the US-Iran conflict pushing oil above $100/bbl. Our headline stance is tactically neutral-to-mildly-bullish with conviction 2/5 (positioning crowding limits near-term upside), while strategically bullish with conviction 3/5 as the carry advantage and growth outperformance should reassert once positioning normalises.

spot.convention: Rising AUD/USD = AUD strengthening; falling AUD/USD = AUD weakening

1m.direction: Modest AUD appreciation; stalled after Iran shock

3m.direction: Strong AUD rally driven by RBA hike repricing

1y.direction: Powerful structural rally from 0.6240 March 2025 lows

bis_reer.interpretation: The REER has normalised higher from the 2020 trough (100 base) but remains well below the 2010–2014 mining boom peak of 120–126. Modestly above fair value but not stretched.

valuation.trade_weighted_assessment: The RBA TWI at 65.5 is above its 2024 average (~62) but below the 2021–2022 peak (~67), consistent with the REER reading of modestly above historical norms reflecting the rate advantage.
valuation.what_is_priced_in: Current valuation levels embed the RBA hiking cycle and broad USD weakness but have not yet priced a terminal rate above 4.10%. The AUD/JPY valuation at +23.8% above PPP is the most stretched bilateral, reflecting the extreme AU-JP rate differential. The market treats AUD as fairly valued vs USD, which is broadly correct.
valuation.actionability: The valuation signal is not actionable as a standalone. AUD is near equilibrium on REER and PPP vs USD — neither deeply cheap nor expensive. The extreme overvaluation vs JPY could drive AUD/JPY mean-reversion if BoJ hiking accelerates, but this is a cross-specific story.

cftc.percentile_context: Maximum bullish extreme on a 3-year COT Index; the COT Index rose 47 percentage points in six weeks, the fastest buildup since H2 2020.

positioning_summary.other_positioning_signals: 25-delta risk reversal at −0.9 vols (AUD puts bid, 9-month low) indicates institutional longs are hedging downside — a fragility signal. Open interest declining from 283K to 267K alongside rising net longs suggests short-covering rather than fresh buying.
positioning_summary.interpretation: Net speculative positioning is at the maximum bullish extreme of the past three years. The COT Index at 100th percentile and the rapid buildup pace represent a meaningful contrarian risk. Declining open interest alongside rising net longs suggests the move is driven by short-covering rather than fresh buying — a less durable dynamic that can reverse quickly.
positioning_summary.contrarian_assessment: Positioning is unambiguously a warning signal but not yet a trigger. The absolute level (+67.8K) remains below the ±80K crowding threshold, but the percentile context (100th) and the pace of buildup (47 ppts in 6 weeks) create asymmetric risk: incremental buying power is limited while unwind potential is significant. A catalyst-driven −2% correction from 0.7063 targets 0.6920, aligning with the prior breakout level.

market_structure_vol_summary.trend_regime: Uptrend with deceleration; consolidation at multi-year resistance 0.7110–0.7135

support.significance: 50-day SMA and 0.70 psychological floor; primary near-term support
Prior breakout level and 100-day MA; dip-buy zone
200-day SMA; CTA trigger level and bull/bear pivot

resistance.significance: February 2023 highs and 2026 YTD high; multi-year resistance cap
2022 post-pandemic high range; upside target on breakout

volatility.rv_vs_iv: RV 10.2% vs IV 11.5% — market overpricing moves by 1.3 pts, indicating event premium exists (IV > RV)
volatility.risk_reversal_25d_1m: −0.9 vols, puts bid — institutional hedging demand concentrated on downside; 9-month low, indicating heavy protective put buying by accounts long AUD spot
volatility.vol_regime: Moderately elevated; transitioning from low-vol grind (pre-February) to event-driven vol (post-Iran shock)

market_structure_vol_summary.key_observation: The most striking feature is the spot-vs-options divergence: spot holds above 0.70 (bullish posture) while the risk reversal at −0.9 vols (9-month low) shows heavy institutional put buying. This dynamic creates a 'spring-loaded' downside if the bullish narrative cracks.

market_context.recent_regime: AUD/USD's regime over the past 2–4 weeks is best characterised as 'consolidation at altitude.' After a sharp rally from 0.6700 in mid-January to 0.7125 in late February — driven by the RBA hike surprise and USD weakness — spot has consolidated in a 0.6990–0.7120 range. The dominant narrative shifted from pure rate support to a tug-of-war between the constructive AUD carry story and rising geopolitical risk premia from the US-Iran conflict. Volatility has risen from depressed early-February levels (VIX ~16, AUD IV ~8.5%) to moderately elevated (VIX ~23, AUD IV ~11.5%), reflecting the new geopolitical regime but not yet signalling panic.

peers.rate_path_next_6m: On hold; hike risk rising for H2

key_spreads.interpretation: Wide — reflects RBA hiking vs Fed on hold; widest since late 2022; primary AUD/USD driver
Positive — supports AUD/NZD above 1.18; widens further if RBA hikes again in May
Very wide — reflects massive RBA-ECB policy divergence; supports EUR/AUD downside
Modest positive — partially offset by BoE's own inflation persistence problem
Extremely wide — underpins AUD/JPY at 35-year highs; most vulnerable to BoJ normalisation convergence

sovereign_yields.curve_shape: AU 2s10s +57 bp (positively sloped); AU 3s10s +38 bp. Both curves positively sloped, signalling the market views the hiking cycle as growth-compatible rather than recessionary.

tactical.analysis: The RBA's Cash Rate at 3.85% ranks 2nd in G10+SGD behind only Norges Bank (4.00%), representing a regime shift from the February 2025 easing cycle when the RBA was cutting alongside peers. The 3 February hike was unanimous (9-0), with the Board concluding that financial conditions 'may no longer be restrictive' — a hawkish assessment that left the door wide open for further tightening. OIS markets price +9 bp for the 17 March meeting (38% probability of a hike) and a full 25 bp hike by the 5 May meeting, implying the market views the next hike as a matter of timing rather than probability. The AU-US 10Y spread at +82 bp is at its widest since late 2022, reflecting the divergent policy paths — the RBA hiking while the Fed holds at 3.50–3.75% with one cut priced by September. This widening rate differential directly supports AUD/USD through both the carry channel and the expectations channel. The 17 March meeting is genuinely live: Governor Bullock's 3 March speech declared 'every meeting is live' and that the Board could 'move more quickly.' The yield curve at AU 2s10s +57 bp remains positively sloped, suggesting the market views the hiking cycle as growth-compatible rather than recessionary. However, the tactical window is complicated by the proximity of the FOMC SEP release on 18 March, which could generate USD volatility independent of AUD fundamentals.
key_data_points: RBA Cash Rate: 3.85% — 2nd highest in G10+SGD (+15 bp below Norges Bank 4.00%)
OIS-implied rate, May meeting: 4.10% (+25 bp from current)
AU-NZ 10Y spread: +27 bp; AU-DE 10Y spread: +211 bp
March meeting probability: 62% hold / 38% hike (+9 bp priced)
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets are pricing a cumulative +25–35 bp of additional tightening to reach 4.10–4.20% by year-end. Our assessment is that the RBA will hike to at least 4.10% and the risk is tilted toward 4.35% given the February SoMP forecast revisions showing trimmed mean CPI at 3.7% mid-2026. The market is underpricing the terminal rate by approximately 15–25 bp, which represents the tactical edge.

strategic.analysis: Over the strategic horizon, the policy rate differential story strengthens. The RBA's February SoMP projects trimmed mean CPI peaking at 3.7% in mid-2026, with inflation not returning to the 2.5% target midpoint until mid-2028 — a notably hawkish forecast set that implicitly requires a higher terminal rate than currently priced. The SoMP's technical assumption of ~60 bp of additional tightening embedded in its forecasts suggests the RBA Staff sees 4.45% as the rate consistent with returning inflation to target. Meanwhile, the Fed is expected to deliver at least one cut by September 2026, and the ECB remains on hold at 2.00%. The BoJ's likely +25 bp hike in H2 2026 (to 1.00%) narrows the AU-JP spread marginally but leaves it at an extreme +285 bp. Over 1–6 months, the RBA's hiking trajectory diverges further from G10 peers, with only Norway running a comparably tight policy. This divergence should widen rate differentials further, supporting AUD through the expectations channel as forward rates increasingly price AUD-positive outcomes. The key strategic risk is that the RBA overtightens into a housing downturn or that a China hard landing collapses the terms of trade, forcing a sharp dovish reversal — but neither scenario is the base case with unemployment at 4.1% and GDP at 2.6%.
key_data_points: RBA SoMP terminal rate assumption: ~4.45% (implied by forecast path)
Fed expected to cut to 3.25–3.50% by end-2026 (1 cut priced)
ECB at 2.00% through H1 2026, hike risk rising for H2
AU-US 10Y spread trajectory: +82 bp now, likely widening to +100–120 bp
AU 10Y yield at 4.97% — highest since July 2011
RBNZ at 2.25% — AUD-NZD policy spread at 160 bp, widest in cycle
strategic.what_is_priced: The market prices RBA terminal at ~4.20%. Our assessment is 4.35% is more likely given the SoMP forecast trajectory, implying ~15 bp of mispricing that accrues over the strategic horizon. If the US economy weakens and the Fed delivers two cuts, the rate differential widens further, amplifying the AUD-positive signal. The strategic edge is in the rate differential widening being underpriced.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical and strategic stances are aligned (both Bullish), but strategic confidence is 5 points higher (75 vs 70). Tactically, the 17 March RBA/FOMC double event, extreme positioning (100th percentile COT), and elevated geopolitical risk create near-term noise that could trigger a 2–3% pullback before the fundamental carry story reasserts. Strategically, these transient headwinds wash out and the widening rate differential becomes the dominant driver.

carry_vs_peers.interpretation: High carry but elevated vol dilutes attractiveness; AUD/JPY carry trade dependent on risk appetite staying constructive
Highest absolute carry in G10; strong carry-to-vol ratio makes AUD/CHF an attractive carry expression
Moderate carry with moderate vol; liquid expression of the RBA-ECB policy divergence
Widest AUD-NZD carry in cycle; low vol makes AUD/NZD the cleanest carry expression in Antipodean complex
Best carry-to-vol ratio in G10+SGD; high carry with very low vol, though SGD's managed NEER limits upside capture
Negligible carry; AUD/USD is not a carry trade — the narrow rate differential means directional moves dominate returns

carry_analysis.carry_regime: Carry has been a strong positive contributor over the past 6 months, with AUD carry trades generating positive total returns across most pairs. The elevated VIX (22.8) creates risk-off headwinds that can temporarily overwhelm carry, but the regime has not yet shifted to one where carry is systematically punished.
carry_analysis.breakeven_move: AUD/USD: annualised carry of +23 bp ÷ 252 trading days × 20 days = +1.8 bp (~0.18 pips). Illustrates that AUD/USD is not a carry trade. AUD/JPY: +310 bp ÷ 252 × 20 = +24.6 bp (~27 pips). Spot can move ~27 pips against the AUD/JPY long before carry is erased over 4 weeks.

tactical.analysis: Australia's inflation and growth data unambiguously support a hawkish RBA and, by extension, a stronger AUD over the tactical horizon. Headline CPI at 3.8% YoY (January 2026) and trimmed mean CPI at 3.4% — the highest in 16 months — confirm that the disinflation process has stalled and reversed. The full monthly CPI provides higher-frequency confirmation of the quarterly trend, and the January reading leaves no ambiguity about re-acceleration. GDP surprised to the upside at +0.8% QoQ and +2.6% YoY in Q4 2025, the strongest in nearly three years, with per-capita GDP positive for four consecutive quarters — decisively ending the per-capita recession. The labour market remains tight: unemployment at 4.1% (below consensus 4.2%), participation steady at 66.7%, and full-time employment surged by +50,500. Wage growth (WPI at 3.4%, public sector 4.0%) continues above the RBA's comfort zone. Leading indicators are mixed but net-positive: Composite PMI at 52.4 (17th consecutive expansion), ANZ Job Ads +3.2% MoM. Consumer sentiment remains pessimistic (Westpac 91.6) but this reflects rate hike anxiety rather than actual weakness.
key_data_points: Judo Bank Composite PMI: 52.4 (February — 17th consecutive expansion)
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has repriced substantially for hotter inflation. The next catalyst is the 25 March monthly CPI print (February data). Consensus expects ~3.6–3.7% headline — anything at or above 3.8% would reinforce the hiking narrative and push March meeting pricing toward 50/50. The market has not fully priced the possibility of back-to-back hikes (February and March).

strategic.analysis: Over the strategic horizon, the inflation-growth dynamic remains AUD-supportive but with increasing two-way risk. The RBA's own SoMP forecasts project trimmed mean CPI peaking at 3.7% in mid-2026 before declining to 2.8% by mid-2027 — a path requiring sustained tightening above 4%. GDP growth is forecast to decelerate from 2.6% to 1.8% by December 2026, reflecting the lagged impact of higher rates. The per-capita recovery provides a cushion — with population growth moderating, per-capita GDP can stay positive even as headline growth slows. The labour market is expected to loosen gradually (unemployment to 4.3% by June 2026) but the starting point is tight enough to support a soft landing narrative. The key strategic risk is that oil above $100/bbl feeds into fuel costs, pushing headline CPI higher than SoMP projections and forcing more aggressive tightening that eventually breaks the growth story. A second risk is China's deceleration (GDP 4.5% Q4 2025, NBS PMIs in contraction) weighing on commodity demand. Strategic confidence is lower than tactical because the forecast path involves growth deceleration that may undermine the carry story later in the horizon.
key_data_points: Per-capita GDP: +0.9% YoY (Q4 2025) — 4 consecutive positive quarters
Oil price: >$100/bbl — upside risk to CPI forecasts
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price growth deceleration from ~2.5% to ~1.8%, broadly consistent with our view. The mispricing lies in inflation persistence: if trimmed mean CPI remains above 3.5% through mid-2026 (~55% probability), the market will reprice the terminal rate higher toward 4.35%, generating another leg of AUD appreciation through the rate channel.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both horizons are bullish, but tactical confidence (75) exceeds strategic (65). Current releases are unambiguously hot, providing high-confidence near-term support. Over the strategic horizon, the growth deceleration forecast and external risks (China, oil) introduce meaningful uncertainty about whether the carry advantage persists or erodes.

tactical.analysis: The risk environment has deteriorated materially since early February and is currently a headwind for AUD, which carries one of the highest betas to global risk sentiment in G10. The VIX at 22.8 (close 9 March, 35+ intraday 10 March) represents a significant regime shift from sub-16 readings of early February. The US-Israel-Iran military conflict is the proximate cause, with Brent crude above $100/bbl and Strait of Hormuz disruption fears elevating geopolitical risk premia. The S&P 500 at 6,796 has breached its 100-day MA and is down ~2% over the past month, while MSCI World fell −2.8% and the Hang Seng dropped −4%. US credit spreads have widened (IG OAS to ~0.98%, HY to ~3.06%) but remain below 5-year averages, indicating stress but not systemic risk. AUD's risk beta means it amplifies these moves: AUD/JPY at 111.5 is elevated but vulnerable to sharp unwind if risk-off deepens. The rate support from the RBA is partially offsetting the risk-off pressure, creating tension where AUD simultaneously receives carry inflows and risk-off outflows.
key_data_points: US IG OAS: ~0.98% (45th percentile vs 5Y avg 1.06%)
US HY OAS: ~3.06% (40th percentile vs 5Y avg 3.50%)
AUD implied risk beta: ~0.7 to S&P 500
tactical.what_is_priced: VIX at 22.8 prices moderate uncertainty but not a tail scenario. If the Iran conflict escalates further (Hormuz closure, direct engagement), VIX could spike to 35–40 sustained, driving AUD/USD 3–5% lower based on historical risk beta. The options market (risk reversal at −0.9) prices tail risk more aggressively than spot.

strategic.analysis: Over the strategic horizon, risk sentiment is genuinely uncertain. Historically, geopolitical risk premia in FX are transient: even the 2022 Russia-Ukraine shock's impact on risk currencies faded within 2–3 months. If the Middle East conflict follows a similar pattern, the strategic impact on AUD is limited because the carry advantage reasserts once vol normalises. However, if oil remains above $100/bbl for 3+ months, the second-order effects (higher global inflation, delayed CB easing, weaker consumer spending) create more persistent risk-off. China equity weakness (CSI 300 −1.9%, Hang Seng −4%) adds a separate risk channel given AUD's China sensitivity. The superannuation hedging dynamic (~$4.3T AUM) provides mechanical support: if global equities recover, super funds rebalance by selling AUD hedges. The net assessment is Neutral: risk is a headwind but unlikely to dominate over 1–6 months given rate support, unless a tail scenario materialises.
key_data_points: Credit spreads: widened but below 5Y averages — no systemic stress
AUD superannuation AUM: ~$4.3T (mechanical hedging flow support)
strategic.what_is_priced: Equity vol markets price moderate uncertainty (VIX 3M ~21) but not sustained risk-off. If geopolitical conflict de-escalates, the risk premium suppressing AUD evaporates quickly — a bullish tail. If it escalates, AUD could underperform 3–5% beyond current pricing. The risk is asymmetric: risk-off is partly priced; risk-on recovery is not.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical bearish (60% confidence) vs strategic neutral (50%). Geopolitical shocks have front-loaded impact on risk currencies like AUD but historically mean-revert over 3+ months. Tactical reflects live escalation risk; strategic reflects the expectation that fundamental drivers dominate longer horizons.

tactical.analysis: CFTC positioning is the single most concerning factor in the AUD setup. Non-commercial net longs at +67,762 contracts represent a 100th percentile reading on the 3-year COT Index — the maximum bullish extreme. The buildup was extraordinarily rapid: +47 percentile points in six weeks. Leveraged funds are net long +54,635 while asset managers are +28,878 — both near the top of 3-year ranges. The decline in open interest from 283,000 to 267,280 is a warning: rising net longs with declining OI indicates short-covering rather than fresh buying, which is less durable. Once shorts are closed, there's no incremental buying pressure. The options market corroborates fragility: 25Δ risk reversal at −0.9 vols shows institutions long in spot are buying downside protection. Historically, AUD positioning at 100th percentile readings has preceded 2–4% pullbacks within 2–6 weeks in 7 of the last 10 instances since 2015. The risk is asymmetric: who is left to buy at the 100th percentile?
key_data_points: COT Index move: +47 percentile points in 6 weeks
Historical precedent: 7/10 instances of 100th percentile preceded 2–4% pullbacks
tactical.what_is_priced: Spot reflects bullish positioning but has not corrected for crowding risk. At 100th percentile, incremental buying power is limited while unwind potential is significant. A −2% correction from 0.7063 targets 0.6920. The market is not pricing positioning unwind risk adequately.

strategic.analysis: Over the strategic horizon, positioning extremes matter less. CFTC data is a mean-reverting signal with a 2–6 week half-life: the 100th percentile reading will normalise through either a pullback or time. The relevant strategic question is the sustainable positioning level given fundamentals. With the RBA hiking while G10 peers are on hold or easing, a structurally net-long position in the 50th–70th percentile is fundamentally justified. The current extreme needs to correct by ~30–50 percentile points to reach sustainable levels, but the directional bias remains long AUD. Asset manager positioning (+28,878, 85th percentile) is more sticky than leveraged fund positions, as real money holds based on fundamentals. The strategic assessment is Neutral because the current extreme creates near-term headwinds but will have normalised within the 1–6 month window, leaving fundamental drivers as dominant.
key_data_points: 3Y COT Index: 100th percentile (will normalise over 4–8 weeks)
Leveraged funds net: +54,635 (more volatile, likely to reduce first)
Asset managers net: +28,878 (more sticky, slower to adjust)
Sustainable positioning range given fundamentals: 50th–70th percentile
Historical half-life of positioning extremes: 2–6 weeks
strategic.what_is_priced: Positioning is a flow factor, not a price factor at the strategic horizon. By 1–6 months, the extreme will have normalised and the market trades on rates, growth, and commodity dynamics. Strategic mispricing is elsewhere.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical bearish (70%) vs strategic neutral (55%). Positioning extremes are a high-confidence near-term contrarian signal with 2–6 week half-life. Over the strategic horizon, positioning normalises and ceases to dominate. The tactical stance reflects timing risk, not a fundamental disagreement with the bullish thesis.
factors.contrarian_analysis_tactical: Positioning is unambiguously stretched at the 100th percentile of the 3-year range — the maximum possible reading. While the absolute level (+67.8K) is below the ±80K historical crowding threshold, the percentile context is more relevant. Three plausible unwind triggers: (1) dovish RBA surprise on 17 March; (2) VIX above 30 sustained from further Iran escalation; (3) below-consensus February CPI on 25 March. Historical analogue: July 2023 unwind from 90th percentile saw AUD/USD fall −3.8% in 10 trading days. The minority contrarian view is that the positioning is 'correct' — structural USD weakness and RBA hiking justify being long. This would be validated by a break above 0.7135.
factors.contrarian_analysis_strategic: Over the strategic horizon, the contrarian framework shifts from 'avoid the crowd' to 'where will the crowd be in 6 months?' Given the fundamentals (RBA hiking, strong growth, wide differential), net spec positioning is likely to remain long but at a more moderate 60th–70th percentile. The risk is a China hard landing or oil-driven recession forcing complete liquidation, reverting positioning to neutral or net short — a 10–15% probability tail. The base case is the positioning extreme corrects via a tactical pullback, then rebuilds to sustainable levels, leaving AUD net-positive over the strategic horizon.

tactical.analysis: Australia's external balance presents a mixed picture with no clear tactical signal. The current account deficit widened to −A$21.1bn (−2.9% of GDP) in Q4 2025 — the widest since Q4 2015 — driven by a primary income deficit of A$21.7bn reflecting foreign investor profit repatriation from mining. The goods and services surplus narrowed to A$1.3bn, and the January trade surplus (A$2,631m) missed expectations. Terms of trade rose modestly (+0.4% QoQ) with iron ore holding above $100/t. The commodity basket is net-stable tactically: iron ore range-bound ($102–106/t), coal elevated ($133–137/t, +31.6% YoY), LNG at $15.71/MMBtu (+18.6% YoY), copper near records ($12,675/t). China is the dominant partner (~30–35% of exports), and NBS PMIs in contraction (49.0) are a watch item — offset by the Caixin divergence (52.1). The external balance is a slow-moving factor with quarterly cadence, providing minimal tactical signal.
key_data_points: Trade balance: A$2,631m surplus (Jan 2026, below A$3,900m expected)
tactical.what_is_priced: The market is looking through the current account deterioration, treating it as compositional (income outflows) rather than structural. This is partially justified. If the February trade balance disappoints further, the market may begin to price external deterioration more seriously. Tactically neutral with limited signal strength.

strategic.analysis: The strategic external balance outlook is the most negative factor in the AUD setup. The current account deficit at −2.9% of GDP is on a deteriorating trajectory. Iron ore faces structural demand decline as Chinese steel production plateaus, property contracts (−17.2% investment in 2025), and EAF adoption accelerates. Simandou ramp (5–10 Mt in 2026) adds seaborne supply. Consensus 2026 iron ore of $90–95/t represents a further 5–10% decline. The strong AUD creates a negative feedback loop: each 1% appreciation reduces export revenue in AUD terms. US Section 122 tariffs (15% on Australian goods, 50% on steel/aluminium) create direct drag, while the indirect China channel (reduced Chinese demand) is the larger concern. Australia's transition from CA surplus (2020–2023) back to structural deficit reflects normalisation the market has been slow to price. The −2.9% deficit is the fifth-weakest in G10+SGD, inconsistent with AUD at multi-year highs.
key_data_points: China property investment: −17.2% in 2025 (structural demand decline)
Trade surplus with China: compressed from US$45bn to US$19bn
strategic.what_is_priced: The market is not pricing external balance deterioration adequately. AUD/USD at 0.7063 reflects the rate story almost entirely. If the deficit widens toward −3.5% of GDP in H1 2026 (plausible given iron ore trajectory and tariffs), the market needs to reprice AUD 2–3% lower on external balance alone, partially unwinding rate-driven appreciation.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical neutral (55%) vs strategic bearish (60%). The external balance is slow-moving: CA data arrives quarterly with a lag, commodity price moves take months to flow through. Tactically, commodity prices are range-bound with no signal. Strategically, structural deterioration (iron ore supply, China demand, tariffs) creates persistent headwinds that intensify over the horizon.

tactical.analysis: The RBA's communication has been unambiguously hawkish since the February decision. The post-meeting statement cited 'material' inflation pickup, faster-than-expected private demand, and greater capacity pressures. The SoMP revised trimmed mean CPI forecasts sharply higher (3.7% mid-2026) with ~60 bp of additional tightening embedded, implying the RBA Staff sees terminal near 4.45%. Governor Bullock amplified the hawkish signal on 3 March, stating 'every meeting is live' and pushing back on needing quarterly CPI before acting. February minutes confirmed financial conditions 'may no longer be restrictive,' housing credit outpacing incomes, and business debt at GFC-era pace. The RBA's credibility remains high — the February hike demonstrated willingness to act against expectations. The gap between market pricing (~4.20% terminal) and SoMP-implied path (~4.45%) represents a 25 bp credibility premium where the market doesn't fully trust the RBA will follow through, creating an asymmetric repricing opportunity.
key_data_points: RBA February decision: +25 bp hike, unanimous 9-0 vote
SoMP trimmed mean forecast revision: 3.7% (Jun 2026, up from 3.2%)
SoMP-implied terminal: ~4.45% vs market ~4.20% (25 bp gap)
Governor Bullock (3 March): 'every meeting is live'
February minutes: financial conditions 'may no longer be restrictive'
Next communication: 17 March decision; 24 March minutes release
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price terminal at ~4.20% vs SoMP-implied ~4.45%, creating ~25 bp underpricing. If 17 March delivers a hike or hawkish hold signalling May, this gap narrows and supports AUD. The risk is that Bullock's language is dismissed as standard rhetoric, but the February hike demonstrated follow-through.

strategic.analysis: Over the strategic horizon, RBA guidance credibility is a sustained AUD-positive factor. The RBA has established itself as the most hawkish G10 central bank, meaning markets price RBA hawkishness with a higher prior, supporting AUD through the expectations channel. The quarterly SoMP releases are the highest-signal events — the May SoMP with updated forecasts will confirm or revise the inflation trajectory. Post-2024 review changes (fewer meetings, press conferences) increase information content per event. The key uncertainty is Bullock's reaction function to housing stress: if dwelling prices fall >10%, a dovish pivot could occur that markets don't price. The RBA's credibility also depends on forecast accuracy; if trimmed mean CPI undershoots the 3.7% mid-2026 forecast, the market would interpret the hiking cycle as a policy error, unwinding rate expectations rapidly. The RBA remains the most credible and transparent G10 central bank in the current environment.
key_data_points: SoMP-implied terminal: ~4.45% vs market ~4.20% (25 bp gap)
Remaining 2026 RBA meetings: Mar 17, May 5, Jun 15, Aug 10, Sep 28, Nov 30
Housing credit growth: faster than incomes (potential constraint)
Inflation forecast risk: trimmed mean 3.7% Jun 2026 — undershoot triggers dovish pivot
strategic.what_is_priced: Market prices a credible but measured RBA reaching 4.10–4.20%. The strategic edge is the ~40% probability the RBA reaches 4.35%+, generating 15–25 bp of additional repricing. The downside — inflation undershoot triggering dovish pivot — would unwind rate advantage rapidly and is the key invalidator.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both bullish; tactically higher confidence (70 vs 65). The RBA's guidance is unambiguous near-term (hawkish statement, governor, minutes). Over the strategic horizon, the credibility of the forecast path becomes the relevant variable, and forecast accuracy is inherently less certain than current communication tone.

tactical.analysis: The geopolitical risk environment is the most elevated since the 2022 Russia-Ukraine invasion, representing a material tactical headwind for AUD as a high-beta risk currency. The US-Israel-Iran conflict has pushed Brent above $100/bbl with Hormuz disruption fears creating persistent oil risk premium. While Australia is a net energy exporter, the commodity benefit is offset by the second-order risk-off impact — AUD sells off during geopolitical shocks regardless of direct commodity exposure. The VIX spike to 35+ intraday demonstrates this. US tariff policy continues creating uncertainty: Section 122 surcharge at 15% replaced struck-down IEEPA tariffs. Australia faces direct impacts (15% goods, 50% steel/aluminium) and indirect impacts via the China channel. The dense central bank calendar (RBA 17 March, FOMC 18 March, BoE/ECB 19 March) compounds the event risk with binary FX outcomes. The options market prices event risk via 1M IV > 3M IV backwardation.
key_data_points: US-Iran conflict: Brent >$100/bbl, Hormuz disruption risk
US tariffs: 15% Section 122 on Australian goods; 50% steel/aluminium
17 March RBA + 18 March FOMC: consecutive day binary risk
Event vol premium: 1M IV (11.5%) > 3M IV (10.8%) — backwardation
Tail risk: Hormuz closure ~10% probability → AUD/USD to 0.6500
tactical.what_is_priced: Options market prices moderate event risk; spot has been resilient. The disconnect suggests spot anchored to rates while options price tail risk. If Iran escalates, spot reprices lower to catch up with options — that repricing is the unpriced risk.

strategic.analysis: The strategic geopolitical matrix is complex and net-negative for AUD. The Iran conflict's impact depends on duration: resolved in 1–2 months = transient; persisting 3–6 months with oil above $100/bbl = material second-order effects (higher global inflation, delayed easing, weaker growth). The tariff overhang is more persistent: Section 122 expires late July, but could be replaced. The indirect China channel (US tariffs reducing Chinese industrial output and commodity demand) is the more important transmission. AUKUS introduces diplomatic uncertainty (CRS report questions Virginia-class delivery). Australia-China trade is stable (all restrictions lifted) but selective tensions persist (BHP iron ore). The May Federal Budget creates domestic event risk. The compound probability of multiple geopolitical risks materialising simultaneously is low (~10–15%) but high-impact.
key_data_points: Iran conflict: persistent >$100/bbl oil for 3+ months = macro headwind
AUKUS: CRS report questions Virginia-class delivery feasibility
May 2026 Federal Budget: fiscal stimulus potential
Compound risk probability: ~10–15% (Iran + tariffs + China tensions)
strategic.what_is_priced: Market prices current geopolitical risk at moderate premium but has not discounted compound risk scenario (Iran + tariff escalation + China tensions). This compound scenario is low probability but high impact (AUD/USD −8 to −12%). The balance of geopolitical risks is tilted downside for AUD.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both bearish; tactically higher confidence (65 vs 55). Tactical reflects the immediacy of the Iran conflict and dense event calendar. Strategic is lower confidence because geopolitical risk premia historically mean-revert over 3+ months, and the base case is gradual de-escalation.

tactical.analysis: This factor is not currently material for AUD and provides no directional signal for the tactical horizon. Australia holds AAA/Aaa sovereign ratings from all three major agencies (S&P, Moody's, Fitch) with stable outlooks — placing it among approximately 11 countries globally at the highest credit tier. Federal gross debt/GDP at 34.2% (A$993bn) and net debt/GDP at 20.1% (A$587.5bn) are among the lowest in the G10, providing significant fiscal headroom that insulates AUD from sovereign risk repricing. The December 2025 MYEFO showed the federal budget deficit narrowing to A$36.8bn, improved by A$8.4bn over forward estimates, consistent with fiscal consolidation supported by strong nominal GDP growth and commodity royalty revenue. Bond supply dynamics are benign with ACGB auctions well-received and no signs of investor resistance to Australian sovereign duration. Victoria remains the most fiscally stressed state with net debt at A$167.6bn (25.1% of GSP) and interest payments exceeding A$10.6bn annually, but sub-sovereign fiscal stress does not affect the national sovereign rating and has no material FX transmission mechanism. No fiscal event exists in the 2–4 week window — the Federal Budget is scheduled for May — no rating agency review is pending, and government bond markets are functioning normally. The sovereign CDS spread is at negligible levels, confirming market consensus that Australia's fiscal position is one of the strongest in the developed world.
key_data_points: Sovereign ratings: AAA (S&P, Fitch), Aaa (Moody's) — all stable
2025-26 deficit: A$36.8bn (improved from A$42.1bn at PEFO)
Victoria net debt: A$167.6bn (25.1% of GSP)
tactical.what_is_priced: Australia's AAA status is fully priced — sovereign CDS at negligible levels, no fiscal risk premium. The market correctly assesses fiscal position as among the strongest in G10. No mispricing in either direction.

strategic.analysis: The strategic fiscal outlook for Australia remains benign with one emerging watch item that does not yet alter the assessment. Gross debt is projected to peak at 37.0% of GDP by 2030-31 — remarkably low by any developed market standard and substantially below the levels that would trigger rating agency concern or market repricing. The May 2026 Federal Budget is the key fiscal event within the strategic horizon: positioned ahead of the next federal election (due by May 2028), it could include significant spending commitments that widen the deficit trajectory, though the current consolidation trend and strong nominal GDP growth provide a buffer against meaningful fiscal deterioration. Victoria's fiscal predicament continues to worsen — net debt surged from approximately A$25bn in 2019 to over A$150bn post-COVID, with roughly A$75bn attributable to pandemic-related spending — but this sub-sovereign stress lacks a direct FX transmission mechanism. The more relevant fiscal dimension for AUD is the relative comparison with G10 peers: Australia's fiscal position at approximately 34% gross debt/GDP compares extremely favourably with the United States at roughly 120%, the United Kingdom at 100%, Japan at 260%, France at 110%, and Canada at 60%. This relative fiscal advantage supports structural AUD overweight in sovereign allocation models, functioning as a quality floor that limits downside rather than a catalyst for upside appreciation. Only a global sovereign debt repricing event — analogous to the UK gilt crisis of September 2022 — would elevate this factor materially, and in that scenario Australia's relative fiscal strength would become a positive differentiator for AUD.
key_data_points: May 2026 Federal Budget: potential fiscal stimulus
Australia vs G10 debt/GDP: 34% vs US 120%, UK 100%, Japan 260%
Fiscal headroom: significant — room for stimulus without threatening AAA
strategic.what_is_priced: No mispricing. Fiscal factor correctly priced as immaterial. Only a global sovereign debt repricing event would change this assessment, and Australia's relative strength would be positive in that scenario.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both neutral with high confidence. The fiscal factor is immaterial for AUD given AAA status and low debt ratios. No divergence between horizons because the trajectory is stable and no events in either window could change the assessment. This is a deliberate 'not material' call.

tactical.analysis: AUD/USD's market structure presents a mixed tactical picture. The trend is structurally bullish (bullish MA stacking: 50d 0.7016 > 100d ~0.6900 > 200d 0.6688, all rising), supporting trend-following capital. However, momentum has stalled: spot range-bound 0.6990–0.7120 for two weeks, failing to break multi-year resistance at 0.7110–0.7135. This creates a consolidation at resistance pattern where either breakout triggers CTA buying or failure triggers stop-loss selling. The volatility picture adds complexity: IV percentile at ~60th is neither cheap nor expensive. The RV vs IV gap (10.2% vs 11.5%) shows an event premium — the market is paying for the 17–18 March central bank meetings. The 25Δ risk reversal at −0.9 vols (9-month low) confirms institutional put demand. The most striking feature is the spot-vs-options divergence: spot holds above 0.70 (bullish) while options price aggressive downside hedging (bearish skew).
key_data_points: 25Δ risk reversal (1M): −0.9 vols (AUD puts bid; 9-month low)
RV vs IV: −1.3 vols (IV > RV — event premium)
tactical.what_is_priced: Options market prices moderate event premium concentrated around March central bank meetings. Risk reversal at −0.9 suggests market assigns higher probability to downside break than upside breakout. Partially inconsistent with spot holding above 0.70. If options traders are correct, spot is mispriced; if spot traders are correct, options protection is cheap.

strategic.analysis: Over the strategic horizon, the market structure tilts moderately AUD-positive, supported by the bullish moving average stacking that acts as a structural tailwind for trend-following and CTA capital. The configuration of 50d (0.7016) above 100d (~0.6900) above 200d (0.6688), with all three averages rising, is a textbook bullish alignment that systematically attracts trend-following flows. Historically, when AUD/USD establishes above the 200-day SMA by 300+ pips with positively stacked MAs, the trend persists for 3–6 months in approximately 65–70% of observed cases since 2010. The key question for the strategic horizon is whether AUD/USD can break and sustain above the critical 0.7110–0.7135 multi-year resistance zone that corresponds to the February 2023 highs. A decisive break above this level would open the technical path toward 0.7300–0.7500, representing the 2022 post-pandemic high range, and would trigger mechanical CTA buying that amplifies the fundamental move by an estimated 1–2%. Failure at this level would establish a double-top pattern suggesting the rally from 0.6240 (March 2025) to 0.7125 is mature. The strategic volatility environment is likely to remain moderately elevated given the persistent geopolitical backdrop (VIX 3M futures trading near 21), meaning realised moves will be larger than the depressed pre-February regime. The 3M implied vol at 10.8% and expected normalisation of the event premium after the March meetings suggest that options become more attractive for strategic expression as the event premium drains, providing cheaper entry into directional options structures.
key_data_points: Spot above 200d SMA by +375 pips (strong bullish signal)
Historical trend persistence: ~65–70% when bullish stacking holds
strategic.what_is_priced: The market structure prices a maturing uptrend at a decision point: breakout or failure at 0.7135. Fundamental drivers favour eventual breakout, but timing depends on near-term catalyst resolution. If breakout occurs, CTA buying would amplify the move by an estimated 1–2% beyond rate-justified levels.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical neutral (60%) vs strategic bullish (55%). The market is at a resolution point (breakout vs failure at 0.7135) that will likely resolve within the tactical window. Strategic benefits from the resolved posture. Strategic confidence is modest because market structure is a secondary factor.
factors.expression_assessment: The IV percentile at ~60th is in no-man's land — not cheap enough (sub-25th) to clearly favour long options, not expensive enough (above-75th) to clearly favour spot. The vol term structure in mild backwardation prices the March central bank events. For a bullish view: the event premium makes short-dated upside options slightly expensive; a spot-plus-short-dated-put expression is more efficient, capturing upside from a 0.7135 break while using puts (the negative risk reversal makes skew favourable for put buyers). For a bearish view: the negative risk reversal means puts are relatively expensive but the skew is directionally aligned; spot short is more efficient given event premium, with a stop above 0.7135. If RV > IV persists post-events, spot expression is favoured over options. Net: wait until March events resolve to reduce the event premium before establishing strategic directional expressions.

tactical.analysis: USD funding conditions are remarkably benign and represent a significant tailwind for AUD. The transition from QT to Reserve Management Purchases (RMPs) on 1 December 2025 proceeded smoothly, with the Fed purchasing ~$40bn/month in T-bills. Reserve balances at $3.02T are up $83bn since RMPs began. SOFR at 3.65% shows no dislocations. The AUD/USD 3M cross-currency basis at +5 to +15 bp is effectively zero to mildly positive — no USD funding premium for Australian institutions, an unusually favourable condition. EUR/USD basis at −15 to −25 bp and JPY/USD at −30 to −50 bp are within post-2020 ranges. USD swap line usage at $0 confirms absence of stress. On the dollar smile, USD is in the weak middle — neither strong from risk-off nor US exceptionalism. This goldilocks USD weakness (DXY −9% YoY) is the sweet spot for AUD. March quarter-end creates mild seasonal funding risk but reserves are more than adequate.
tactical.what_is_priced: Market prices smooth continuation of post-QT liquidity. The risk is an unexpected liquidity event (T-bill supply surge, quarter-end squeeze) that temporarily widens AUD/USD basis — ~10% probability with 1–2% AUD impact. The edge is modest: the post-QT transition reduces future USD liquidity shock probability relative to 2023–2024.

strategic.analysis: The strategic USD liquidity outlook is net-positive for AUD with one significant uncertainty. The Fed's reserve management approach (~$40bn/month in T-bills) prevents a repeat of the 2019 repo dislocation. Reserve balances at $3.02T are above the estimated minimum (~$2.5–2.8T). The ON RRP draining to $0 was absorbed without dislocation. The strategic uncertainty is Fed Chair nominee Kevin Warsh, who has expressed hostility toward the bloated balance sheet — if confirmed, he could push for resumed QT or faster RMP rundown, tightening USD liquidity and strengthening USD. This is not base case but introduces a 6–12 month tail risk (~15% probability). On the dollar smile, the strategic outlook is for USD to remain in the weak middle: US economy growing but not outperforming (GDP 2.2% vs Australia 2.6%), global risk elevated but not crisis-mode, Fed expected to ease. This goldilocks environment is the structural base case for AUD strength.
key_data_points: Fed Chair nominee: Kevin Warsh (balance sheet risk factor)
Dollar smile position: Middle (weak USD — AUD sweet spot)
Fed expected to cut 1x by Sep 2026 → further USD weakening
strategic.what_is_priced: Market prices continued benign USD liquidity with the Fed in a 'quiet' phase. The unpriced risk is a Warsh-driven policy shift toward balance sheet reduction, which could strengthen USD by 3–5% over 6–12 months. We assign ~15% probability within the strategic horizon. The base case (85%) is benign liquidity persists, supporting AUD through broad USD weakness.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Both bullish; tactically higher confidence (65 vs 60). Tactical benefits from certainty that USD liquidity is benign now. Strategic incorporates Warsh tail risk and possible dollar smile regime shift. Directional alignment reflects the dominant trend of broad USD weakness.

weighted_score.interpretation: Mildly bullish. The positive score reflects dominance of Policy (+0.168) and Inflation/Growth (+0.135), which together contribute +0.303. Offsetting bearish factors (Positioning −0.070, Risk −0.060, Events −0.033) sum to −0.163, dampening the fundamental signal.
weighted_score.mapping_to_conviction: Net score +0.261 maps to Conviction 2/5 — a mild directional lean that does not warrant aggressive positioning.

tactical.rationale: The tactical AUD setup is a textbook case of strong fundamentals meeting poor timing. The two dominant factors — Policy & Rates (+0.168 contribution) and Inflation & Growth (+0.135) — are unambiguously bullish: the RBA at 3.85% with terminal pricing of 4.10–4.20%, trimmed mean CPI at 3.4% (16-month high), GDP at 2.6% (near-3-year high), and unemployment at 4.1% (below consensus). These fundamentals alone contribute +0.303 to the weighted score and justify AUD at or above current levels. However, three tactical headwinds conspire to limit near-term upside and create meaningful pullback risk, collectively contributing −0.163. First, CFTC positioning at the 100th percentile of the 3-year range means the marginal buyer is absent — the market is maximally long, and any negative catalyst triggers an asymmetric unwind given that open interest is declining (283K to 267K, suggesting short-covering rather than fresh buying). Second, the US-Iran conflict has elevated the VIX to 22.8 (spiking to 35+ intraday on 10 March), creating a risk-off overhang for AUD's high-beta characteristic that suppresses the carry attraction. Third, the dense event calendar — RBA on 17 March, FOMC with SEP on 18 March, BoE and ECB on 19 March — introduces binary risk that could move AUD ±2–3% depending on outcomes. The path of least resistance over 2–4 weeks is a modest pullback toward 0.6900–0.6950 that resets positioning from the 100th to the 75th–80th percentile before the carry trade can re-engage at more attractive levels. The 17 March RBA decision is the primary catalyst: a hike or strongly hawkish hold would validate the bullish thesis, while a dovish hold would trigger the positioning unwind. The 18 March FOMC SEP is the secondary catalyst: a dovish shift with more cuts signalled would weaken the USD and support AUD/USD. Conviction is limited to 2/5 because the near-term signal is conflicted — the right fundamental trade (long AUD) has poor tactical entry risk at positioning extremes and elevated geopolitical volatility. This view is actionable for risk management (reduce existing long AUD exposure, tighten stops to 0.6900) but not for new position initiation at current levels.

g10_ranking.commentary: AUD's tactical ranking is suppressed by the positioning extreme — on fundamentals alone it would rank 2nd or 3rd, but crowding risk demotes it to 4th. It beats low-yielders (EUR, CHF, JPY) and same-rate peers with weaker growth (CAD), but loses to NOK (higher rate, better external balance), GBP (comparable rates, less crowded), and temporarily to USD (safe-haven demand) and NZD (less crowded, better contrarian risk/reward).

conditions: RBA holds in March with hawkish guidance
base_case.path: Positioning partially resets from 100th percentile toward 75th–80th. AUD/USD dips to 0.6950 support, then stabilises near 0.7000–0.7050.

conditions: RBA hikes 25 bp on 17 March to 4.10%
RBA holds with unexpected dovish tilt

tactical.path_expectation: The most likely tactical path is a modest pullback followed by consolidation. AUD/USD likely dips toward 0.6950–0.7000 in weeks 1–2 as positioning unwinds ahead of the 17 March RBA meeting. The RBA decision sets near-term direction: a hike targets 0.7100+; a neutral hold targets continued range. The FOMC on 18 March adds a secondary impulse. Volatility will be elevated (10–12% annualised) with daily ranges of 50–80 pips. A break above 0.7135 signals the pullback is over; a sustained break below 0.6900 signals a deeper correction.

increase_to_5_if: RBA hikes to 4.10% in March AND FOMC signals 3+ cuts
CFTC resets below 60th percentile AND VIX below 17 AND iron ore above $110/t
increase_to_4_if: AUD/USD breaks above 0.7135 on strong volume
CFTC positioning resets below 80th percentile AND VIX below 20
decrease_to_3_if: VIX normalises below 20 without positioning reset
decrease_to_2_if: Positioning remains at 100th percentile with no catalyst resolution
decrease_to_1_if: VIX sustains above 30 for 5+ days AND oil above $110/bbl
RBA delivers dovish hold with explicit pause signal

risk_reward.risk_reward_ratio: 1.0:1 (approximately symmetric — upside and downside scenarios roughly equal in magnitude and probability)
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Neutral. Positioning extreme creates downside asymmetry (more room to fall on unwind), but rate differential creates upside pull (carry accrues daily). These offsetting forces produce a roughly symmetric distribution.

weighted_score.interpretation: Moderately bullish. The strategic score is higher than tactical (+0.288 vs +0.261) with healthier composition. Bullish contributors (Policy +0.180, Guidance +0.078, USD Liquidity +0.072, Inflation/Growth +0.065) total +0.395, offset primarily by External Balance (−0.096) and Event Risk (−0.028).
weighted_score.mapping_to_conviction: Net score +0.288 maps to Conviction 3/5 — a clear directional stance warranting moderate position sizing.

strategic.rationale: The strategic AUD case is built on a durable rate advantage that is likely to widen further over 1–6 months. The RBA's hawkish trajectory (3.85% heading to 4.10–4.35%) against a backdrop of G10 peers mostly on hold or easing creates the widest AUD carry opportunity in over a decade — a structural shift from the 2024 environment where the RBA was cutting alongside peers. The AU-US 10Y spread at +82 bp is likely to expand toward +100–120 bp as the Fed delivers at least one cut while the RBA hikes further, providing a powerful magnet for yield-seeking capital flows through both the carry and expectations channels. The USD liquidity backdrop amplifies the rate story: the post-QT transition to Reserve Management Purchases keeps funding conditions benign, and the broad USD weakness (DXY −9% YoY) provides the goldilocks middle-of-the-smile environment where high-beta commodity currencies like AUD thrive. Central bank guidance reinforces the rate advantage — the RBA's February SoMP implies a terminal rate ~25 bp above current market pricing, creating an information edge that accrues gradually as the market reprices toward the RBA's own forecast path. Factor interactions are largely reinforcing: rates, guidance, and USD liquidity all point in the same direction (bullish AUD), while inflation/growth data validates the RBA's hawkish stance. The principal offsetting factor is the external balance deterioration: the current account deficit at −2.9% of GDP on a worsening trajectory, with iron ore consensus drifting toward $90–95/t and Simandou adding new seaborne supply, creates a structural headwind that limits the extent of AUD appreciation. This external balance drag is why conviction is 3/5 rather than 4/5 — the rate story is powerful but the commodity and current account narrative provides a fundamental ceiling that prevents a full-conviction bullish stance. The cross-horizon consistency is notable: tactically we expect a positioning-driven pullback, but strategically the fundamental carry story reasserts, targeting AUD/USD 0.7200–0.7350 by mid-2026. The catalyst for the transition from tactical caution to strategic conviction is the resolution of the March event risk and normalisation of CFTC positioning from the 100th percentile extreme. The trading implication is clear: use any tactical pullback toward 0.6900–0.6950 as the strategic long entry point with a stop below the 200-day SMA at 0.6688.

g10_ranking.commentary: AUD's strategic ranking improves from tactical 4th to strategic 3rd as the positioning headwind fades and rate advantage dominates. AUD ranks behind NOK (higher rate, fiscal surplus, oil tailwind) and GBP (comparable rates, less external vulnerability). AUD ranks above NZD because the AU-NZ rate differential at 160 bp is the widest in years with Australian growth materially outperforming.

conditions: Iran conflict de-escalates to manageable tension
base_case.path: Pullback to 0.6900–0.6950, then grind higher through 0.7135 by April–May, consolidation at 0.7200–0.7300 by June.

conditions: China stimulus accelerates (iron ore back to $110+)

strategic.path_expectation: The strategic path is a 'pullback-then-grind' pattern. Phase 1 (weeks 1–4): AUD/USD corrects 1–3% toward 0.6900–0.6950 as positioning extremes unwind and March event risk resolves. Phase 2 (months 2–3): fundamental carry reasserts as RBA hikes to 4.10%, market reprices terminal higher, positioning reset allows fresh buying. AUD/USD grinds back through 0.70 and tests 0.7135. Phase 3 (months 4–6): if 0.7135 breaks, CTA buying drives a move toward 0.7300–0.7400. Volatility remains moderately elevated (10–12% annualised) with periodic 1–2% drawdowns. Key pullback support: 0.7000, 0.6900 (100d MA), 0.6688 (200d SMA, critical pivot).

increase_to_5_if: RBA hikes to 4.35% AND Fed cuts 2x AND China major stimulus
DXY falls below 98 AND AUD/USD sustains above 0.7300
increase_to_4_if: RBA hikes to 4.10%+ AND iron ore holds above $95/t
CFTC resets to 60th–70th percentile AND AUD/USD breaks 0.7135
decrease_to_2_if: Iron ore falls below $85/t AND China GDP below 4.0%
Current account widens to −3.5% of GDP
decrease_to_1_if: RBA pauses/cuts AND oil sustains above $120/bbl
AUD/USD breaks below 200-day SMA (0.6688) for 3+ days

risk_reward.risk_reward_ratio: 0.4:1 raw pips; probability-adjusted: E(gain) 50% × 187 pips = +93.5 pips vs E(loss) 25% × 463 pips = −115.8 pips → 0.8:1
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Modestly skewed against the long position. Downside scenarios produce larger absolute moves than base case upside, reflecting AUD's high-beta character. Partially offset by daily carry accrual (~0.5–1.0% over 6M). Net: risk/reward is acceptable but not compelling — requiring half-size positioning and strict stops below 200d SMA at 0.6688.

consensus_divergence.consensus_view: Median bank forecast for AUD/USD year-end 2026 is 0.70–0.71, with Westpac at 0.71, NAB at 0.71, ING at 0.69, and RBC at 0.70. Most forecasts were published when AUD/USD was trading 0.63–0.67 in late 2025/early 2026.
consensus_divergence.our_view: AUD/USD 0.7200–0.7300 by mid-2026, with upside skew toward 0.7400 if the RBA hiking cycle extends beyond 4.20%. We are 2–3% above the median consensus for the same timeframe.
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: Consensus forecasts are stale — set before the RBA hike to 3.85% and the broad USD weakening. Most bank models have not updated for: (a) the SoMP forecast revisions implying terminal rate ~4.45%, (b) accelerating AU-US rate divergence (Fed cutting, RBA hiking), (c) DXY structural weakness (−9% YoY), and (d) the offsetting coal/LNG price strength that partially compensates for the iron ore decline narrative anchoring consensus lower.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: If AUD/USD reaches 0.7300 against consensus 0.71, the outperformance is ~190 pips or ~2.7% — a meaningful alpha opportunity for FX portfolios.
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: If AUD/USD reverts to consensus 0.7050, the loss from entering at current spot is ~13 pips or ~0.2% — negligible.

cleanest_bullish_expression.rationale: The AU-NZ rate differential at 160 bp is the widest in the cycle; Australian growth (2.6% GDP) vastly outperforms NZ (1.3%); NZ faces a twin-deficit problem (CA −5.7% of GDP). AUD/NZD at 1.1900 has broken the 2024 range decisively. Lower volatility than AUD/USD and mean-reverting tendencies make it a cleaner expression of bilateral fundamentals without USD noise.

cleanest_bearish_expression.rationale: If the bearish scenario materialises (risk-off, BoJ hiking, AUD carry unwind), AUD/JPY at 111.50 (near 35-year highs, +23.8% above PPP) offers the most asymmetric downside. The AU-JP rate differential at 310 bp is extreme and would compress rapidly in a risk-off environment as BoJ hikes and RBA eventually pauses.

relative_value_pairs.thesis: Rate and growth divergence; widest AU-NZ policy spread in cycle; cleaner than AUD/USD for expressing RBA outperformance.
Equal policy rates (3.85% vs 2.25% — AUD carries +160bp) with Australian growth outperforming Canada; AUD/CAD breaking above 0.95 for first time in years.
Norges Bank rate higher (4.00%), Norway has massive CA surplus (+14% of GDP) vs Australia deficit (−2.9%); external balance convergence favours NOK.

cross_horizon_consistency.summary: Tactical neutral (2/5) and strategic bullish (3/5) diverge — a common 'right trade, wrong timing' setup.
cross_horizon_consistency.divergence_explanation: The tactical caution is driven by transient factors — positioning at a 3-year extreme (100th percentile COT) and elevated geopolitical risk (VIX 22.8, Iran conflict) — that have a 2–6 week half-life. The strategic bullish stance is driven by structural factors — rate differentials, growth outperformance, USD weakness — that persist over 1–6 months. The tactical headwinds do not invalidate the strategic thesis; they create adverse entry conditions.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: Late March to early April 2026, when: (a) 17–18 March central bank meetings resolve, (b) CFTC positioning resets from 100th to 75th–80th percentile, and (c) VIX normalises below 22.
cross_horizon_consistency.catalyst_for_transition: The 17 March RBA meeting is the primary catalyst. A hawkish hold or hike validates the strategic thesis and likely triggers the positioning reset. A dovish surprise extends the tactical caution phase.
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Do not initiate new long AUD positions at current levels and positioning extremes. Wait for a pullback toward 0.6900–0.6950 (100-day MA, prior breakout) to establish strategic longs with a stop below 0.6688 (200-day SMA). Use the tactical pullback as entry for the strategic trade.

edge.1m: −11 to +11 pips (forward 0.7061 vs expected 0.6950–0.7050; near-term noise)
edge.3m: +42 to +142 pips (forward 0.7058 vs expected 0.7100–0.7200; rate divergence accruing)
edge.6m: +132 to +282 pips (forward 0.7068 vs expected 0.7200–0.7350; significant edge)

forward_vs_spot.interpretation: The forward curve is nearly flat at short tenors (narrow AUD-USD rate gap) but tilts toward AUD appreciation at 6M as the market prices RBA-Fed divergence. Our expected rate at 6M (0.7200–0.7350) is significantly above the 6M forward (0.7068), implying a 132–282 pip edge. This is the quantifiable opportunity justifying the strategic long — the forwards are not pricing the full extent of rate differential widening.

key_catalysts.rationale: A BoJ hold is expected but any hawkish signal would narrow the AU-JP spread, pressuring AUD/JPY. A dovish BoJ supports AUD/JPY carry trades. The 310 bp AU-JP differential makes this meeting relevant for the risk barometer cross.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Fully — hold expected with no change to forward guidance

key_catalysts.rationale: Market prices 38% hike probability. A hike to 4.10% would validate the hawkish narrative and likely drive AUD/USD above 0.7100. Even a hold with hawkish guidance ('May is live') would be mildly supportive. A dovish hold would be bearish and trigger a positioning unwind.

The dot plot update is critical: if the median dot shifts from 2 cuts to 3 for 2026, USD weakens broadly and AUD/USD rallies. If dots shift hawkish (1 cut or hold), USD strengthens. The consecutive-day RBA/FOMC creates compounding vol potential.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — 1 cut by Sep priced; 2 cuts would be dovish surprise

key_catalysts.rationale: Employment data confirms or challenges the tight labour market narrative. A print showing unemployment at or below 4.1% supports the RBA hiking case. Consensus expects 4.1–4.2%.

First full monthly CPI since the RBA hike. A hot print (>=3.7%) reinforces the May hike expectation; a cooling print (<=3.5%) defers it. Given the SoMP's 4.2% headline forecast for June, anything above 3.7% confirms re-acceleration.

NBS PMIs have been in contraction (49.0 manufacturing) but Caixin diverges (52.1). March should normalise for Lunar New Year. Recovery above 50.0 supports the China-AUD growth narrative and commodity demand outlook.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Not priced — market focused on domestic data

key_catalysts.rationale: RBNZ at 2.25% expected to hold. Any dovish language or indication of cuts widens AU-NZ spread, supporting AUD/NZD above 1.19. A hawkish RBNZ surprise (unlikely) would narrow the differential.

China's GDP trajectory is critical for AUD through the commodity channel. Consensus expects 4.5–4.8%. A strong reading validates 'resilient China'; a weak reading amplifies iron ore demand concerns. The Caixin-NBS divergence makes this release a potential clarifying event.

key_catalysts.event: ABS Monthly CPI (March — includes Q1 Quarterly CPI)
key_catalysts.rationale: The single most important AUD data release in the strategic horizon. The Q1 quarterly CPI directly feeds the RBA's May 5 meeting and May SoMP forecasts. A hot print could trigger an immediate May hike; a cooling print defers tightening.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Not yet — market awaits February CPI first

key_catalysts.rationale: The May meeting includes the quarterly SoMP with updated forecasts. If trimmed mean CPI forecasts are revised higher (from 3.7% mid-2026), the terminal rate repricing extends. Market prices a full 25 bp hike to 4.10%.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — full 25 bp hike to 4.10% priced for May

key_catalysts.rationale: Pre-election budget could include spending commitments that widen the deficit but support short-term growth. Market will assess whether fiscal expansion complicates the RBA's inflation fight (hawkish for rates) or signals growth slowdown requiring support (dovish).
Confirms whether Q4 2025 growth acceleration (+2.6% YoY) is sustainable. A strong reading supports the RBA tightening rationale. Consensus expects modest deceleration to ~2.2–2.4%.

key_catalysts.event: ABS Monthly CPI (June — includes Q2 Quarterly CPI)
key_catalysts.rationale: The Q2 CPI determines whether the RBA's 3.7% trimmed mean mid-2026 forecast is on track. An undershoot would trigger repricing for earlier peak rate and possible easing pivot — the key inflection for the AUD bullish thesis. An overshoot extends the hiking cycle.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Not yet — too far forward for market to price

invalidators.condition: RBA Cash Rate expectations fall below 3.60% (market prices reversal of February hike)
invalidators.mechanism: Would signal the RBA's own data assessment was wrong and the hiking cycle was a policy error, removing the primary fundamental pillar of AUD strength and triggering rapid unwinding of carry positions.

invalidators.condition: Iron ore 62% Fe falls below $80/t for 2+ consecutive weeks
invalidators.mechanism: Sub-$80 iron ore would compress Australia's terms of trade by ~8–10%, push the current account deficit toward −4.0% of GDP, and signal material China demand destruction exceeding the structural decline narrative.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: SGX 62% Fe Iron Ore futures front month

invalidators.condition: VIX sustains above 35 for 5+ consecutive trading sessions (not intraday spikes)
invalidators.mechanism: Sustained high vol represents a regime shift to risk-off where AUD's high beta becomes a liability, overwhelming the rate support as carry trades are liquidated across the G10.
invalidators.quantitative_threshold: VIX close above 35 for 5+ consecutive sessions

invalidators.condition: Australian unemployment rate rises to 4.5% or above
invalidators.mechanism: Would signal the labour market is loosening faster than the RBA's SoMP forecasts (4.3% mid-2026), potentially triggering a dovish pivot that removes the rate support underpinning AUD.

invalidators.condition: AUD/USD closes below the 200-day SMA (0.6688) for 3+ consecutive days
invalidators.mechanism: A sustained break below the 200d SMA would trigger CTA selling, break the bullish trend structure, and signal that fundamental and technical factors have aligned to the downside.

invalidators.condition: China GDP growth falls below 4.0% YoY for any quarter
invalidators.mechanism: Would represent significant deceleration from 4.5–5.0% trajectory, implying material downside to Australian commodity export volumes, iron ore prices, and the current account balance.

invalidators.condition: Strait of Hormuz physically closed to shipping for 48+ hours
invalidators.mechanism: Oil would spike to $130–150/bbl, VIX would surge above 40, and the risk-off impact would overwhelm any commodity benefit to Australia, driving AUD/USD toward 0.6200–0.6500.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: Shipping tracker data, oil prices, news

signposts.threshold_confirms: Holds above $95/t through Q2 2026 (terms of trade stability)

signposts.indicator: CFTC AUD Net Speculative Positioning (COT Index, 3Y)
signposts.threshold_invalidates: Rises above +80,000 absolute contracts (extreme crowding → reversal)

signposts.threshold_confirms: Remains >=3.3% through mid-2026 (validates RBA hiking rationale)
signposts.threshold_invalidates: Falls below 3.0% (disinflation resumes → RBA pause/cut risk)

signposts.threshold_confirms: Settles below 20 (risk-off headwind fades, carry resumes)
signposts.threshold_invalidates: Sustains above 30 for 5+ days (risk regime shift against AUD)
Narrows below +40 bp (convergence → carry advantage eroding)

signposts.threshold_confirms: Remains above 51.0 (private sector expansion supports commodity demand)
signposts.threshold_invalidates: Falls below 49.0 (contraction → iron ore/copper demand destruction)

seasonality.pattern: March-April typically sees AUD/USD strength from Q1 commodity contract resets and repatriation flows. August often sees AUD weakness from earnings season hedging and superannuation rebalancing flows.
seasonality.relevance_window: March-April seasonal strength falls within the tactical window; August seasonal weakness falls within the strategic window.
seasonality.historical_magnitude: Q1 seasonal tailwind typically contributes +0.5–1.0% to AUD/USD performance; August hedging drag typically −0.5–1.5%.
seasonality.current_alignment: The Q1 seasonal pattern is modestly supportive of the tactical setup, consistent with the fundamental carry story. The August seasonal headwind could coincide with the RBA's August meeting/SoMP and create a pullback opportunity within the strategic horizon.

assumptions: Policy rate comparisons use the stated target rate (not effective rate); the Fed is quoted as the midpoint of the target range (3.625%).
OIS-implied rates are estimated from market commentary and RBA Board meeting probability assessments rather than directly observed OIS curves, introducing ~5–10 bp uncertainty.
PPP fair values use OECD GDP-based PPP estimates (2024 vintage), which are approximate and should be interpreted as directional guidance rather than precise targets.
CFTC data is lagged (Tuesday report date, Friday release); actual positioning may differ from reported levels by the valuation date.
Implied volatility and risk reversal levels are estimated from market commentary rather than live market data, introducing ~0.5–1.0 vol uncertainty.
BIS REER data is available with 1–2 month lag; January 2026 is the latest available.
Carry calculations use policy rate differentials as a proxy for actual carry; basis-adjusted carry would differ by the cross-currency basis swap level.
Cross-currency basis levels are estimated ranges reflecting difficulty sourcing live basis swap data outside institutional terminals.
China GDP and PMI data are subject to measurement uncertainty; the NBS-Caixin divergence is noted as an analytical challenge; we weight Caixin more heavily for private sector assessment.
